---
title: "Lab session: Representation learning"
jupyter: 
  
  kernelspec:
    display_name: Python 3 (ipykernel)
    language: python
    name: python3
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
description: Representation learning lab.
execute:
  eval: true
---

## Running on colab

You can use this
[link](https://colab.research.google.com/github/NBISweden/workshop_NN_DL/blob/rnn/labs/airplanes.ipynb)
to run the notebook on Google Colab. If you do so, it's advised that
you first make a copy to your own Google Drive before starting your
work on the notebook. Otherwise changes you make to the notebook will
not be saved.


## Preparation

### Configuration

Execute the following code blocks to configure the session and import
relevant modules.

```{python}
%config InlineBackend.figure_format ='retina'
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2
%matplotlib inline
```

```{python}
import os
import sys
import math 
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader, Subset
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler
from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

from sklearn.datasets import fetch_lfw_people
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
from sklearn.metrics import ConfusionMatrixDisplay, classification_report
from sklearn.model_selection import RandomizedSearchCV, train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.svm import SVC
```

## Lab session: shallow representations

We'll start with one of the fundamental methods for learning representations: Principal Component Analysis (PCA). PCA is a linear method that finds the directions of maximum variance in the data, and projects the data onto those directions. This can be useful for dimensionality reduction, visualization, and as a preprocessing step for other machine learning algorithms.

### Loading the data

We will use the Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW) dataset, which contains images of faces collected from the web. The task is to classify the images based on the identity of the person in the image.
```{python}
lfw_people = fetch_lfw_people(min_faces_per_person=70, resize=0.4)

# introspect the images arrays to find the shapes (for plotting)
n_samples, h, w = lfw_people.images.shape
print("Total dataset size:", n_samples, "images of size", h, "x", w)
```

Let's have a look at the images and the targets in the dataset:

```{python}
X = lfw_people.data
y = lfw_people.target
target_names = lfw_people.target_names
n_classes = target_names.shape[0]
print("Number of classes:", n_classes)
print("Target names:", set(target_names))
```

And a few example images:
```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 5, figsize=(15, 6))
for i, ax in enumerate(axes.flatten()):
    ax.imshow(lfw_people.images[i], cmap='gray')
    ax.set_title(target_names[y[i]])
    ax.axis('off')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

We will treat each of these images as a vector, by just flattening the 2D image into a 1D vector. This is a common preprocessing step for many machine learning algorithms. <video src="RepresentationLearningScene.mp4" width="500" controls>


```{python}
from pathlib import Path
p = Path(" RepresentationLearningScene.mp4")
print(p.exists())
```
